Second Kings 15 presents a relentless catalogue of instability, violence, and spiritual decline that demands your attention precisely because it refuses to look away. The chapter documents the collapse of Israel's northern kingdom through a succession of assassinations and coups—Zechariah murdered by Shallum, Shallum by Menahem, Pekahiah by Pekah, and finally Pekah by Hoshea. Each ruler is introduced with the same damning refrain: he did evil in the sight of the Lord and did not turn away from the sins of Jeroboam. This is not random chaos or the mere mechanics of power politics. This is the inevitable fruit of sustained rebellion against God. Meanwhile, Judah under Azariah (also called Uzziah) and then Jotham appears outwardly more stable—longer reigns, construction projects, doing what is right in the Lord's eyes—yet even here the text reveals a hollow compromise. The high places remain standing. The people continue their idolatry. Right action and persistent disobedience exist side by side in the same kingdom. This should unsettle you more than the straightforward brutality of Israel's chaos.

The violence in this chapter is shockingly specific and deliberately included. Menahem's attack on Tiphsah includes the barbaric detail that he "ripped open all the pregnant women." This is not footnote material. It is what the kingdom of Israel looks like when it has abandoned the God who gave it law and covenant. The steady parade of rulers grasping for power through assassination, the foreign tribute extracted from the wealthy to pay off Assyrian kings, the steady military losses and deportations—all of this flows from a single source: the refusal to worship the Lord alone. Israel's kings did not stumble into this darkness accidentally. They inherited and perpetuated "the sins that Jeroboam son of Nebat had caused Israel to commit." Two centuries earlier, Jeroboam had erected golden calves to replace pilgrimage to Jerusalem and the true worship of God. By chapter 15, that original sin had metastasized into the complete dissolution of order, justice, and human dignity. When you reject God's revealed way, the structures of your society do not simply bend—they break.

Yet even as Israel fractures into violence and Assyrian armies march into Galilee to take captives, the Lord's word to Jehu is being fulfilled with mathematical precision. "Four generations of your sons will sit on the throne of Israel," God had promised, and here, in verse 12, the text pauses to remind you that Zechariah's death marks the completion of that promise. This is not random. This is not the triumph of Assyrian military power alone. The God who rules over the kings of Assyria is orchestrating judgment with sovereign control. Judah, meanwhile, receives a strange mercy and a quiet warning. Jotham does what is right, yet the high places remain. He builds the Upper Gate of the Lord's house, yet the people continue sacrificing elsewhere. And then, in a parenthetical remark that opens the door to the next chapter's crisis, the text notes that "in those days the Lord began to send Rezin king of Aram and Pekah son of Remaliah against Judah." Jotham's partial obedience—righteousness without wholehearted reform—does not protect him from the gathering storm. He inherits stability, but his son Ahaz will inherit war.

What strikes deepest in this chapter is not its narrative spectacle but its patient documentation of cause and effect. Sin compounds. Compromise calcifies. And through it all, God's word stands. The Lord is not absent from the fall of kingdoms. He is not caught off guard by assassins or powerless before empires. He judges. He fulfills His promises. He withdraws His protection from those who persistently reject Him. As you read this parade of failed rulers and fallen cities, ask yourself: where in your own life does compromise linger? Where do you do some things right while tolerating persistent disobedience in other areas? The text shows you that such inconsistency is not stability. It is the foundation cracking beneath your feet, and only genuine, whole-hearted repentance and trust in God's mercy can save you from the judgment that inevitably follows.
